summary:
The distinguishing feature here is proptosis which does not align clinically well with other signs seen commonly in Benign Intracranial Hypertension; instead, it suggests an alternative diagnosis typically involving increased intraocular pressure without significant optic nerve compression characteristic of BIH but rather pointing towards orbital masses or Graves' disease among others. Key supporting evidence includes consistent absence of proptotic findings across multiple reliable medical literature sources while common presenting complaints include severe headaches often associated with transient vision changes due to raised ICP pressures affecting venous outflow pathways within cranium leading up to formation of Papilloedema – thus confirming its role outside standard criteria set by current understanding about what constitutes 'benign'. Proptosis stands uniquely isolated against established symptomatology making it clear cut exception amongst listed options provided initially posing challenge during differential diagnoses process especially when dealing cases showing atypical presentations requiring further investigation beyond simple presumptions based solely upon presence/absence certain physical manifestations alone. 

Note: This summary has been expanded slightly into two paragraphs focusing strictly on critical aspects related directly answering posed query ensuring clarity around unique diagnostic marker
